text:
This guy had zero coding experience and AI background. Today, he makes over $300K a month with AI automation.

Meet Nate Herk.

Here's how he built a business without writing a single line of code:

1. Built a foundation without realising it

During a college internship, he learned workflow logic, data processing, and how to turn messy inputs into clear outputs. He didn’t realise this at the time, but this was his head-start.

2. Mastered one skill

Instead of trying to "learn AI," Nate focused on automation. He taught himself tools like n8n, learned how APIs worked, and built simple workflows repeatedly until he got good.

3. Committed to content while working full time

He made one promise to himself: post on YouTube consistently, no matter what. His daily system was simple - learn something, build something, record it, post it. He did this while holding down a full time job.

4. Quit his job with less than 500 subscribers

With fewer than 500 subscribers, businesses started reaching out. He realised he didn't need cold outreach. By consistently showing up online, he was attracting the exact people who needed his help.

5. Turned services into an agency

At first, he did everything himself: Sales calls, building AI automations, client delivery. Those projects taught him what businesses would actually pay for. He hired a team, built systems, and scaled.

Today, his agency generates around $100K/month.

6. Productised his knowledge

As his audience grew, people wanted to learn AI automation from him. So he launched a membership community. He simplified the experience, focused on retention, and built around what members actually needed.

Today, that community generates over $200K/month.

And his three business units - YouTube, education, and agency - make him over $300K per month combined.

The barrier to AI automation isn't coding. It's picking one skill, showing up every day, and refusing to quit before it works.

Do you think you need a technical background to make it in AI?

hashtag#AI hashtag#founders hashtag#automation#startups

If you keep looking for one AI tool like it’s "THE ONE”, your outputs will mostly stay average.

Because AI doesn’t work like that.

Different AI models are optimised for completely different strengths. And once you understand that, your workflow changes completely.

At NB Media, we have been trying generating a lot of AI influencer content.

Initially, we tried using the same model for everything. But the output never really worked for what we needed. So we changed the approach.

Here’s the stack we actually use now:

1. For images → ChatGPT (DALL·E 3)

For us, this gave the most production-ready outputs consistently.

Especially for:
-Human expressions
-Cleaner compositions
-Better lighting & colour control
-More natural-looking generations

Most of our AI video content is generated here.

2. For video → Gemini (Flow / VO3)

We tried doing video generation inside ChatGPT too. It didn’t work for our use case.

But for:
-Motion consistency
-Scene sequencing
-Camera movement
-Tone across shots

Gemini performed significantly better. So we stopped forcing one tool to do both.

3. For building & coding → Claude

Claude became our backend brain.
We use it for:
-Structuring projects
-Testing automations
-Debugging workflows
-Connecting APIs & MCP servers

It simply handles technical reasoning better for our workflows.

The biggest mistake people are making with AI right now: They’re choosing tools like they’re choosing a life partner.

One tool. One subscription. One ecosystem.

But that usually leads to average results.

You wouldn’t ask your video editor to design thumbnails, write backend code, and run paid ads.
So why expect one AI model to do everything well?

Are you still using a single AI tool for most of your work?

hashtag#AI hashtag#tools hashtag#entrepreneur hashtag#founders

If you're a lawyer, a CA, a recruiter, or a consultant...you can now sell your expertise as software.

Without writing a single line of code.

For years, software businesses belonged to people who knew how to build apps.

That's changed. AI assistants used to just answer questions. Now they can follow your exact process and do the work for you.

Which means your expertise - the way you think, the frameworks you use, the checklists you've built over years - can now be packaged and sold as a software product.

People would pay for it because they don't want generic AI answers. They want proven ways of thinking.

And here's how you can sell it:

1. Document your process

↳ Write down exactly how you do the work - what information you collect, what questions you ask, what red flags you look for. Think of it like training a junior employee to think exactly like you.

For example: A lawyer who reviews contracts would write down every clause to look for, every red flag that matters, every question to ask before signing.

2. Turn it into a Skill.md file

↳ Format your document as a Skill.md file - a structured text document that tells an AI assistant how to approach a task using your exact methodology. Add examples of good outputs so the AI knows what a strong result looks like.

3. Publish and collect payments

↳ Upload it to ClawHub - the App Store for AI assistants. Buyers discover it, pay via Stripe, plug it into their AI assistant, and start using your process immediately.

Skills like these sell for $10 to $50 per download. If 100 people buy it, that's $1,000–5,000 generated from expertise you already use every day.

You don't need a team. You don't need a developer. You just need a process that other people would pay to replicate.

If you had to package one part of your expertise into a Skill, what would it be?

hashtag#skills hashtag#AI hashtag#business hashtag#founders

This guy built a $100K/month AI SaaS with zero coding experience.

Meet Albert Olgaard. He has no computer science degree or developer background.

His product, Shiny.Ai , helps businesses and agencies build AI agents without needing technical skills.

Here's how he built it:

1. Started with services, not software

He started by building websites, CRMs, AI voice agents and lead generation systems for local businesses.

After solving the same problems for enough clients, he realised some of those solutions could become software products.

2. Used AI to build the first version himself

Albert had no coding background. So instead of spending thousands on engineers, he built the first version himself using tools like Claude Code.

He used website and dashboard templates, added payments and authentication, and launched the product in about a month.

3. Turned a client problem into a product

Albert wasn't guessing what customers wanted. He had spent months helping businesses with CRMs, automations, lead reactivation and AI agents.

So by the time the startup launched, he already knew businesses were willing to pay for the outcome.

4. Used content as his distribution channel

Instead of relying on ads, Albert documented what he was building. Tutorials. Agent demos. Workflows. AI use cases.

Over 8 months, he grew to 240,000+ followers. That audience became his first users, feedback loop and distribution channel.

5. Got users first. Perfected the product later.

Instead of spending a year adding features, he got the product into users' hands quickly and listened to what they actually wanted.

Their feedback shaped and helped improve the product, and showed him which features were worth building next.

Today, Shiny.Ai has 1,600 paying users and generates over $100K/month.

What business problem would you build software for today?

hashtag#founders hashtag#AI hashtag#startups hashtag#SaaS

If you're passionate about content, social media growth, and building brands online, we'd love to hear from you.

📍 Mohali
📩 Apply now or tag someone who'd be a great fit.

hashtag#Hiring hashtag#SocialMediaManager hashtag#ContentCreation hashtag#YouTube hashtag#DigitalMarketing hashtag#NBMedia

This engineer sold his startup for $90 million, then built Notion into a $10 billion company.

Meet Akshay Kothari, COO of Notion, one of the fastest-growing productivity tools in the world.

His story is about two bets that made no sense at the time.

1. Built an iPad news app in 6 weeks

Stanford gave students 11 weeks to build a product. Akshay built Pulse, a news-reading app.

Weeks after launch, Steve Jobs called it out by name at Apple's WWDC keynote. It went on to hit 30 million users in 3 years.

2. LinkedIn paid $90 million for it

In 2013, LinkedIn acquired Pulse. Akshay joined as VP of Product and Head of LinkedIn India.

3. Walked away from a VP title to join an 8-person startup

He had invested in Notion early and stayed close to founder Ivan Zhao. In 2018, Ivan asked him to come on as COO. He said yes to the risk.

4. Built every business function from scratch

Sales. Marketing. Support. Finance. Legal. Akshay built all of it from the ground up, function by function.

5. Notion nearly collapsed under its own growth

When COVID hit, users flooded in overnight. But the entire product ran on a single database. The team had weeks before things fell apart.

They fixed it.

6. From 8 people to a $10 billion company

- 8 people to 1,000+
- Revenue up 100x
- 20 million+ users worldwide

Akshay took two bets most people would never consider. Both paid off.

His story shows us:
Bet on the right people, and always ship before you're ready.

What's your biggest takeaway from Akshay's story?

hashtag#startups hashtag#founders hashtag#business

This founder built a €35K/month AI agency with no outreach or sales team.

Meet Linus Meister Magsino. He built his entire business around one simple gap:

AI software companies are great at selling tools - but terrible at helping customers actually use them.

Here's how he turned that into a business:

1. Made content around one AI tool

Linus started posting simple tutorials about an AI voice platform. The founder saw that Linus could actually help his customers and brought him on as a consultant.

2. Found the real problem

Users were struggling to make the platform work within their business:

→ CRM integrations → Workflow automations → API connections → AI setup

None of them had the technical skills to do this themselves. That was the gap.

3. Became the setup partner for those users

Linus started helping companies who subscribed to the platform but couldn't set it up. Then the platform team started referring high-value customers to him directly.

4. Turned referrals into a system

Thanks to Linus, the B2B customers used the tool more. He also offered a 20% revenue share back to the platform for referrals.

Now, they started pitching his service to all their customers and sent him more deals.

5. Scaled up with fixed packages

→ €1K basic setup
→ €2K integration package

And in 4 months:
→ 150+ projects completed
→ €30-40K/month revenue

The real opportunity here isn't building the next AI tool.

It’s becoming an AI Implementation Consultant for a niche, high-value tool.

Would you explore this path yourself?

hashtag#AI hashtag#business hashtag#founders hashtag#consultant

We stopped paying ₹2,40,000/year on external tools and built our own instead.

Because we realised mass-market tools can never solve your exact problems.

We started with our HR platform. Discontinued the subscription, rebuilt it internally, and connected it directly to YouTube, ClickUp, and our bonus tracking.

Then came the bigger problem.

We needed a tool that could transcribe a YouTube video and analyse the visual content at the same time - what's being said AND what's happening on screen, together, as one output.

No tool in the market did both. You'd get transcription or visual analysis. Never together.

So we built that too.

Then a QA tool that cut review time from 6 hours to 20 minutes.

Then a subtitle tool hitting 93% accuracy when the market standard sits at 80-85%.

This is something an agency should be doing. But there's a long-term investment we’re making as well.

We're now training our own internal AI model.

Right now, AI costs are low. But companies like OpenAI are losing money. Once the bubble bursts, those costs are going up - and every agency that depends entirely on external models is going to suffer.

When that happens, we'll have an in-house model we control and rely on.

Building internal tools isn't just about saving money today. It's about not being held hostage tomorrow.

Are you building in-house or still fully dependent on external tools?

hashtag#AI hashtag#tools hashtag#founders hashtag#agency

India makes over a billion business calls every day. Most of them are still handled by humans.

One Bengaluru startup just raised $6.3 million to change that.

Meet Bolna (YC F25) AI - built by Maitreya Wagh (ex-Bain) and Prateek Sachan (ex-Zomato).

They noticed India never moved to chat and email the way the rest of the world did. Bank collections, lead qualification, delivery confirmations - it all still runs on humans.

But building voice AI for India turned out to be harder than anywhere else. Here's what they figured out:

1. India's language problem is unlike any other

An Indian caller will open in Hindi, mix in English, and slip into their dialect - all in one sentence. Bolna built for 10+ Indian languages, including mid-conversation switching.

2. No single AI model is good at everything

OpenAI handles reasoning well. But for Indian accents and dialects, Sarvam AI - an Indian company that built its models specifically for Indian languages - does it better. Bolna automatically picks between them on every call.

3. It has to be cheap - or it’ll never scale

They’re priced very competitively at ₹7/min, dropping to ₹3/min at scale. That math only works if you're not burning expensive AI on every call - which they aren't.

The traction has been hard to ignore:
- $6.3 million raised
- 1,050+ paying customers
- 1,500 to 200,000 calls per day in under 8 months

YC once told them "Indian enterprises won't pay for this."

The founders proved them wrong, applied 5 times, and got accepted as well.

Do you think voice will outpace text as India's primary AI interface?

hashtag#AI hashtag#founders hashtag#startups hashtag#business

A guy with zero coding experience built an AI SaaS that automates dental diagnosis reports. And made over $100K from it.

Meet Ben. He's not a developer. Didn't know how to code before starting. He just saw a painful problem in dentistry and decided to figure it out anyway.

Here's exactly how he did it:

▶️ 1. Stopped chasing startup ideas

Instead of inventing the "next big app," he looked for industries drowning in repetitive manual work.

Dentistry kept coming up. Clinics were spending hours manually writing treatment plans and diagnosis reports. That became the opportunity.

▶️ 2. Validated the problem before building anything

Most people spend months building products nobody asked for. He did the opposite.

He cold called dentists and asked one simple question: "If this existed, would you actually use it?" Those conversations shaped the entire product.

▶️ 3. Built only the core output first

He didn’t go for any fancy dashboard, polished UI or complex features.

Just: upload X-ray, AI analyses it, treatment report gets generated. That was enough to test if it solved a real problem.

▶️ 4. He used AI tools to replace an engineering team

His stack cost less than $100/month:

→ Cursor for vibe coding
→ GPT/Claude for debugging
→ Lovable for UI generation
→ Make.com for automation
→ Supabase for database
→ Render for deployment

▶️ 5. He learned while building

He didn't disappear for two years to "become technical." He broke things, fixed bugs, watched tutorials, asked AI dumb questions, and repeated that every single day.

That's the part most people skip.

The barrier to building software is no longer coding - it’s finding a painful problem and having the courage to start.

If you understand the problem deeply, today AI can help you build the solution faster than ever.

What niche problem do you think is sitting right in front of someone?

hashtag#AI hashtag#SaaS hashtag#automation hashtag#founders

Most people trying to sell AI automation will make $0.

Because businesses don’t pay for “AI agents.” They pay for workflows that save time or make money.

And these are the 5 workflows they keep buying:

▶️ 1. Instant lead response systems

↳ A lead fills out a form on a website. Within seconds, they get an automated WhatsApp or email asking qualifying questions and the sales rep gets notified with all the details.

↳ Best for: Dental clinics, real estate, law firms, agencies.

▶️ 2. Invoice and document processing

↳ Invoices arrive in a company inbox as PDFs. AI reads them, extracts the vendor name, amount, and date, and pushes that data straight into their accounting software or CRM without manual data entry.

↳ Best for: Accounting firms, insurance, logistics, legal teams.

▶️ 3. Follow-up and nurture sequences

↳ Someone attends a webinar or fills an enquiry form but never books a call. The system automatically sends personalised WhatsApp or email follow-ups over the next few days until they either reply, book, or opt out.

↳ Best for: Coaches, consultants, agencies, SaaS, B2B service providers.

▶️ 4. Database reactivation

↳ Businesses usually have thousands of dead leads sitting inside CRMs. AI sends personalised outreach referencing their previous interaction and interested leads are automatically flagged for the sales team.

↳ Best for: Gyms, SaaS, e-commerce, dental clinics, subscription businesses.

▶️ 5. Automated reporting dashboards

↳ Instead of someone manually pulling numbers from their CRM, ad dashboard, and spreadsheets every morning - this workflow does it automatically and delivers a clean daily or weekly summary straight to email.

↳ Best for: Agencies, sales teams, startups, any company running multiple tools.

The best part?

Every single one of these can be built with n8n or Make.

→ Use n8n if you want lower costs and don't mind a slightly steeper learning curve.
→ Use Make.com if you want to land your first client fast and learn as you go.

Which of these 5 would you build first?

hashtag#AI hashtag#business hashtag#founders hashtag#startups

The next wave of unicorn businesses will be built on AI-agents.

If you’re building that future, this company has $10 million ready to support you.

They’re called Hyperagent, and their logic is hard to argue with.

A 5-person team fully leveraged by agents can now operate at the scale of a 100-person company. That’s today, so imagine what can happen in 5 years.

Their platform helps you run these agents. And they’re supporting 500 founders with $20,000 worth of credits each to help you build your agent-led company.

Yes, they’re doing it as a marketing stunt. But if you’re building something like this anyway, getting 1 seat out of 500 can change your life.

Just imagine what you could build with ₹19 lakh in AI credits.

You can apply on their website before the deadline on 31st May.

Do you think this is a marketing gimmick, or will it actually help you?

hashtag#AI hashtag#agents hashtag#founders hashtag#startups

This founder raised $21 million, had to shut down his own company overnight, and rebuilt from scratch.

Today, his product is used by Fortune 500 companies, and he's growing 40% month on month.

Meet Tanay Kothari - founder of Wispr Flow, an AI tool that turns your natural speech into formatted text directly inside any app.

Here's how he did it:

1. Grew up chasing one idea

Growing up in Delhi, Tanay had the idea to build tech that felt as natural as talking to a close friend. That obsession eventually took him to Stanford.

2. Tried building it before the world was ready

At 18, he built one of the earliest voice assistants - before Siri and Alexa became mainstream. It hit 2.5 million users in 6 months. But Google shut it down with a cease-and-desist.

3. Raised $21 million for a different idea

Years later, Tanay and his Stanford roommate raised money to build a Bluetooth earpiece that could convert silent speech into text without saying a word out loud.

4. Found out he was building the wrong thing

Tanay walked into the office and saw his entire team using an internal voice-to-text tool instead of the hardware they had spent years building. That was the moment he realised the real opportunity was the software.

5. He made the hardest call of his career

Overnight, they scrapped the Bluetooth earpiece entirely. Rebuilt Wispr Flow as a software product from scratch and launched it to the public in 8 weeks.

6. Silicon Valley couldn't stop talking about it

2 to 10,000 users in 30 days - purely word of mouth. VCs were already using it. That led to raising $81 million and a post-money valuation of $700M.

7. Then Tanay came home

India was always the next chapter. Hinglish support, Android launch, ₹320/month pricing.

Their long-term goal is eventually bringing it down to ₹10-20/month - because Tanay wants voice AI to reach far beyond just premium users.

Today, India is Wispr’s second-largest market after the US. And the company says it retains nearly 70% of users even after 12 months.

From getting shut down by Google to finally building the voice product he dreamed about as a kid, Tanay stayed obsessed with the same problem for nearly two decades.

What do you think of his journey?

hashtag#AI hashtag#founders hashtag#startups

We built a system that tells every editor exactly how much bonus they’ve earned - in real time.

And it changed how our creative team thinks about performance.

Earlier, bonuses were calculated manually.

Our HOD would go through every video, identify editors, pull YouTube views, calculate payouts - across multiple channels. It worked, but it was slow and exhausting.

It worked. But it was slow, painful, and honestly not a great use of anyone's time.

So we built an internal system that connects everything together automatically.

Here's how the system works:

-It pulls task data directly from ClickUp to identify who worked on which video
-It pulls performance data directly from YouTube analytics
-Then it cross-references both datasets automatically
-Based on the views, it calculates each editor’s bonus instantly

The logic is simple. More views, more bonus. You perform better, you earn more.

This quarter, we’re also factoring in CTR and deadlines - so it rewards both quality and speed.

Every editor will now know exactly where they stand at any point in the quarter.

No waiting for reviews or guessing if the system is fair. The data is visible in real time.

And when people have clarity, they usually become far more motivated.

We’re rolling it out fully next quarter, and I’m genuinely excited to see how the team responds.

How does your agency currently track and reward creative performance?

hashtag#bonus hashtag#automation hashtag#editors hashtag#performance

One of my writers walked up to me and said, "Nikit Sir, I can’t do this anymore.”

I didn't see that coming. Priya was one of the best writers on the team. So I asked her why.

"I like writing, but… it’s the research.

Every single day, I spend hours just trying to find what I need. Court websites, news articles, hours of footage. By the time I've found everything, I'm already exhausted."

I spoke to the rest of the team and I found out that they felt the same way. We produce documentary style content covering real cases - and the research that goes into every single video is overwhelming and exhausting.

So we decided to fix it.

We spent a month building a tool on Gemini API that does the entire research process automatically:

-It pulls full case details from across the internet
-Tracks court dates and flags conviction updates in real time
-Scrubs through hours of footage and timestamps the exact moments worth using

Everything lands in one place, ready for the writer to start immediately.

A few weeks in, we went back to the team for feedback.

The response was unanimous. They were finally spending their time on the part of the job they actually loved. The writing.

Also Priya (who wanted to quit) still works with us, and is much happier now.

What's the most time consuming part of your team's day that AI could fix?

P.S. Name is changed for privacy.

hashtag#ai hashtag#tool hashtag#research hashtag#content

This engineer from a Tier 2 college is now the CTO of Anthropic.

Rahul Patil studied at PES Institute of Technology in Bengaluru. But even without the stamp of an Ivy League university, he spent the next 20 years building his skillset.

Here’s how that took him to the CTO seat at Anthropic:

1. Moved to the US for his masters

He left Bengaluru for a Master's in Computer Science at Arizona State and did an MBA at University of Washington after that. All before his career even began.

2. Joined Microsoft and stayed for nearly a decade

While peers were jumping companies, he spent years mastering distributed systems and large-scale infrastructure - the kind of work most engineers avoid because it's unglamorous and complex.

3. Got his first leadership role at Amazon

He managed the Kinesis Firehose team at AWS - the system that processes real-time data streams for the world's biggest companies. His first taste of leading something critical at scale.

4. Earned his first CTO title at Stripe

He moved to Stripe in 2020 and worked his way to CTO - running global engineering for a platform processing over a trillion dollars a year, at 99.999% uptime.

5. Then Anthropic came calling

They needed someone who could make Claude faster, cheaper, more reliable, and enterprise-ready at scale. Patil had spent 20+ years building exactly that expertise so he became their obvious choice.

For decades, India was seen just as the world’s back office for tech.

Today, an engineer from a Tier 2 college is leading the one of the most advanced AI companies in the world.

What do you think of his journey?

hashtag#engineer hashtag#AI hashtag#career hashtag#success

We integrated AI into every stage of our production process. It cut our turnaround time in half - without replacing a single person.

At one point, every video was taking almost 30 days to go from idea to upload. This stopped us from reaching our goal of hitting 220 million views in 2025.

So instead of telling the team to “work harder” this year, we audited the entire production pipeline to figure out where the time was actually going.

Here's what we found:

→ Research: writers spending 3+ hours per case before writing a single word
→ Subtitles: QA team spending a full day per video checking and correcting captions
→ Thumbnails: our artist spending half a day on research before design even started
→ Quality checks: multiple rounds of manual review at every stage

Each stage had its own bottleneck. And each one was eating days we didn't have.

So we built AI tools to fix each one:

-A research tool that pulls full case details, court dates, and footage timestamps automatically
-A subtitle engine built on Gemini API that hits 93-94% accuracy - more than the ones in the market
-A thumbnail tool that handles all research and title generation before the artist opens Photoshop
-A QA tool that cuts review time from a full day to half a day per video

The result: 30 days down to 15 days per video.
Same team. No new hires. Just a faster system.

I think a lot of founders misunderstand how AI changes a business.

The biggest advantage isn’t replacing people. It’s helping them spend less time on repetitive work that slows everything down.

Because most delays in a company come from 50 small tasks that quietly eat a few extra hours every single day.

What's the one stage in your production process that eats the most time?

hashtag#ai hashtag#production hashtag#workflow hashtag#timesaving

We built an AI tool to do our thumbnail artist’s job.

Except… we didn’t replace her. We made her 2x faster.

Before this, half her day was gone before she even opened Photoshop.

Her process looked like this:

- Manually searching for what's trending on YouTube
- Studying competitor thumbnails one by one
- Brainstorming titles from scratch
- Then finally starting on the actual thumbnail

So we built an internal AI tool to handle all of that in minutes:

- Gemini scans YouTube for what’s trending in our niche
[Best AI for analysis]

- Claude generates 10–20 title variations ranked by click potential
[Best AI for creative writing]

- ChatGPT turns those into thumbnail concepts with reasoning behind each one
[Most efficient AI for simple logical tasks]

- Then our tool pulls competitor references automatically

Now she just reviews, picks a direction, and goes straight into creation.

No extra hires. No drop in quality.

This is just one of the internal tools we've built. Across the board, we save 200+ hours a month this way.

Not by replacing people. But by removing the parts of their job that were never meant to be manual in the first place.

What’s the most time-consuming part of your content process right now?

hashtag#ai hashtag#tools hashtag#content hashtag#systems

I'm looking for someone to quit their 9-5 and travel the world to make videos with me.

No, not as a travel buddy. But as my personal videographer.

For context, I'm the founder of NB Media. We're an AI-driven content agency that specialises in Instagram and YouTube.

And I'm looking for someone to document me growing our 8-figure media company.

Here's what the role actually looks like:

- Travelling with me to events, shoots, meetings, and even international trips
- Keeping the visual aesthetic of my personal brand consistent across everything
- Bringing ideas for Reels, Shorts, and vlogs - not just pointing a camera

One day you're sitting in on a boardroom meeting in Mumbai. The next, you're on a flight to cover a shoot in another city. No two days look the same.

And tbh, I don't care how much experience you have.

But you do need an eye for storytelling, instincts for framing and light, and the reflex to capture a moment before it's gone.

So if you've shot college fest after-movies, short films, or freelance reels - and the work speaks for itself - that's all I need to see.

If you get the role: you travel with me (I cover your flight, stay, meals), do what you love, and get a masterclass in entrepreneurship that no college will ever teach you.

If you’re interested, comment 'VIDEO'.

I'll DM you the application link.

P.S. Send me a connection request too so I can reach you easily.

hashtag#hiring hashtag#videographer hashtag#foundersoffice

We ditched every subtitle tool in the market - even the ones that hit 85% accuracy.

That sounds like overkill, until you realise that for us, a wrong word in a subtitle isn't just an editing error. It can completely change the meaning of what someone said.

We couldn't afford that risk. So we built our own subtitle tool and got it to 93–94% accuracy in one week.

Here’s how:

We started with Whisper AI because it's what most production companies use locally.

Spent weeks testing it. Different prompts. Different workflows. Different pipelines. But couldn’t push it beyond 80–85% consistently.

Which meant: 15–20 wrong words every 100 words.

At scale, that became a massive editing bottleneck for our QA team.

So instead of forcing the tool to work, we rebuilt the system using Gemini API. The difference was impressive.

But accuracy was only part of the problem.

Every subtitle tool formats captions differently:
-Random line breaks
-Inconsistent timing
-Subtitles that look terrible on mobile

So we built a standardisation layer on top of the AI:

→ Fixed character limits per line
→ Balanced timing between cuts
→ Controlled line breaks for readability
→ Standardised caption sizing across formats

This is why we invest in building our own tools. The market wasn't solving our problem. So we did.

Every video that goes out now saves our QA team half a day of work.

Have you ever built something in-house because existing tools weren’t good enough?

hashtag#ai hashtag#tool hashtag#systems hashtag#founders

We built an AI tool that cuts video review time from 6 hours to 20 minutes.

Before this, my team was spending that time reviewing every single video before it went live.

-Flagging visual glitches and clip transitions
-Checking every subtitle word by word
-Verifying every name, date and fact
-Identifying speaker labels on screen

This bottleneck was stopping us from scaling.

So we built an AI QA (quality assurance) tool.

Here's how it works:

You upload the video. The AI does the rest.

→ Scans every subtitle against the audio automatically
→ Flags every factual inconsistency for review
→ Catches visual glitches and jitter in transitions
→ Checks speaker labels across the entire video

One third of our QA team's productivity - recovered.

Building this taught us something important. The bottleneck in a content business is rarely the creative work. It's everything around it - the checking, the verifying, the reviewing that has to happen before anything goes live.

This is also one of several AI tools we've built in-house at NB Media. We're still testing it internally across all our channels. Once we've proven it works consistently, we'll open it up to other agencies.

If a tool like this would be of use to you, comment “QA”.

I’ll share access as soon as it’s ready.

This IIT student turned down $525,000 and a Stanford PhD to build his own startup in his dorm room.

Meet Varun Vummadi, co-founder of Giga - it builds AI agents that handle business calls from start to end, without the need of a human.

And here's how he did it:

1. Came from nothing

Varun grew up with very limited means, often struggling to make ends meet. But he went on to crack IIT Kharagpur - and later won over $100,000 in Kaggle coding competitions.

2. Got the offers most people dream of

A $525,000 quant trading role and a Stanford PhD seat - the kind of offers that make your entire family feel like the struggle was finally worth it.

3. Said no to all of it

Varun and his IIT batchmate Esha turned down a combined $675,000 in offers to build a company together. Their families were furious. But they built it anyway.

4. They spotted the opportunity early

They saw the global shift toward voice AI coming and believed it was the right moment to build. So in 2023, they moved to San Francisco and started Giga.

5. The bet started paying off

They got into YC, raised $61M. YC's Jared Friedman called them one of the "first great AI companies to come out of India.”

6. Then things got ugly

Right after the funding, accusations of toxic culture hit publicly. Then an anonymous group threatened to leak 70GB of data unless Giga paid $3 million in crypto.

7. Varun chose transparency over silence

Instead of settling quietly, he posted the extortion email publicly, denied everything, and notified law enforcement. And kept building Giga.

As of today, both Varun and Esha are actively working on new product updates in voice AI and made it to Forbes 30 Under 30 Asia.

From Kaggle contests to $61 million, this isn't an overnight success story. It's what happens when you back yourself so hard there's no safety net left to fall back on.

What do you think of his journey?

AI companies are making you dependent on their tools on purpose.
And when they 10x the price, you won't be able to say no.

That $200 subscription wasn't priced that way because it costs $200 to run. It was priced to get you hooked.

And when it becomes $2,000 - because it will - you won't have a choice. By then, your entire business will be built on it.

The difference with AI is the dependency is being built faster than anything we've seen before.

The internet bubble took almost a decade to burst. AI won't take that long because companies are integrating these tools into their core operations in months, not years.

And here's what will happen when the pricing spike comes:

They'll lose their ability to function. Because they didn't just outsource their execution. They outsourced their thinking.

That's the part nobody is talking about.

The people who will survive a pricing spike will be the ones whose work doesn't stop when a tool disappears.

The ones who spread across multiple tools, upskilled their teams, and understood the principles well enough to adapt.

So use AI tools as aggressively as you want. Just make sure your business can still survive without them.

If your most used AI tool doubled its price tomorrow, what would you do?

Aarohi & Avni started posting 2 months ago, and they already have 700K+ followers. But they don’t even exist.

I know the creator who built this account, and he told me exactly how he did it.

Here's the process he followed:

1. Built the character's prompt using ChatGPT

He used a Custom ChatGPT with an AI UGC plugin. Fed in a reference image - pose, outfit, background. It wrote the full prompt covering skin texture, lighting and setting.

When something looked off, he asked for the prompt in JSON format, edited just that one element and regenerated instead of starting from scratch.

2. Generated the image in Google Flow

He fed that prompt into Google Flow with the character's face as a reference. He looked for two things - natural skin texture and slight imperfections. He kept regenerating until it felt like an actual person.

3. Generated the video and voice after that

For movements, he used Higgsfield motion control. For speaking and ad content, he used Veo inside Google Flow. He gave them a dialogue and Veo generated a consistent voice automatically - no separate voice cloning tool needed.

After 2 months of Build → Test → Post → Repeat:

-700K+ followers
-100M+ views from a single video
-Closed brand deals worth ₹5L+
-Celebrity DMs “OMG they look so real”

AI influencers are already here, already growing, already making money. And the window to get in early is still open.

Would you try building one?

After 5 years of running NB Media, I realised I'm a terrible manager.

And honestly, it was the best thing I ever figured out about myself.

For years, I was doing what I thought a founder was supposed to do. Weekly reports. Team check-ins. Tracking deadlines. Managing people. I was doing all of it, but I was never enjoying any of it.

I was burning out and couldn't figure out why. So I reached out to my mentor Ronit. He sat me down and told me:

"You're someone who builds things from scratch. Stability and managing people drains you because that's not who you are.”

I sat with that for a while. And the more I thought about it, the more I realised he was right.

Because when I'm honest with myself, I love starting things. Building from zero. Experimenting with new ideas and directions. That's where I come alive.

But managing people day to day, chasing reports, resolving conflicts - that was never me. And the more I forced myself into that role, the further I got from the work I actually loved.

That's when it hit me that this was never a workload problem. It was a self-awareness problem.

I really wish someone had pulled me aside on day one and asked - what kind of founder are you, really? Not what kind do you think you should be.

So I'm asking you that now.

Are you a builder or an operator?

A mechanical engineer with no MBA just became CEO of the world's most valuable company.

Here's what John Ternus did differently to climb to the top of Apple:

1. Didn't just focus on academics in college

He competed in swimming while studying mechanical engineering at UPenn. It took focus away from his studies at times, but it built discipline in a way a classroom never could.

2. Started solving real problems as a student

For his final year project, he built a mechanical feeding arm that people with quadriplegia could control using head movements. Gave himself real-world experience.

3. Chose a small company over a big name

After graduating, he joined a small startup building VR headsets instead of chasing a brand-name employer. Smaller companies move faster, and he could learn without waiting for approvals.

4. Never said no to boring work

Then, he joined Apple, where his first task was counting the grooves on a screw head with a magnifying glass. He thought “Is this normal?” - but he did it anyway.

5. Spent a decade in the background

For over 10 years, he quietly with no public visibility. He was absorbing Apple's culture - the perfectionism, the focus on quality.

6. Never asked for responsibility - just kept earning it

In 2020, he was handed iPhone hardware engineering - Apple's biggest business at $200B+ a year. It was a scary role, but he took it on and made it work.

7. Became the youngest on Apple's executive team

And led the Mac's full transition from Intel to Apple Silicon. One of the most complex engineering bets in Apple's history.

8. Stayed with one company for 25 years

Learning from the best, and quietly building trust from the inside.

That's how a mechanical engineer with no MBA ends up running a $4 trillion company.

As someone who’s an engineer-turned-CEO too, I love this story.

My degree didn't build NB Media. But the problems I tried solving, my businesses that failed, the skills I learnt along the way… they all did.

Degrees open doors. But they don't build companies.

What do you think of his journey?

This Harvard dropout reached a $1.5B unicorn valuation in under 2 years - without pitching a single investor.

Meet Jesse Zhang, co-founder of Decagon - it builds AI agents that answer customer support questions - across chat, email, voice, and SMS.

Here's how he went from being a dropout to building a unicorn:

1. He left Harvard with no backup plan

Studied computer science at Harvard. But he was more interested in building than staying in class, so he graduated a year early and started his first company.

2. His first startup taught him everything

He built Lowkey, a gaming startup later acquired by Niantic. But Jesse has said those years were chaotic - he was working hard without knowing what was actually worth building.

3. He met his co-founder at a retreat

In 2023, Jesse met Ashwin Sreenivas at a founders retreat - a fellow founder with a previous exit. When GPT-4 launched, instead of rushing to build, they spent weeks talking to potential customers, just to find a problem worth solving.

4. One expensive problem kept coming up

Enterprises were spending millions on support teams answering repetitive queries. Everyone wanted one thing: automation. Investors said it was crowded. Jesse ignored them.

5. So they built Decagon

Decagon automates customer support across chat, email, voice, and SMS. But the real difference was that it actually worked for complex queries. Not just basic chatbot replies.

6. They built exactly around that pain

For the first 6 months, they didn’t guess features. They built directly from customer requests. Early users like Vanta shaped the product. Which meant faster results, and nothing got wasted.

7. That’s what created pull

They hit 7-figure ARR in 6 months. So instead of chasing investors, investors came to them with pre-emptive offers. Only then did they raise at a $1.5B valuation.

Most founders chase investors. Jesse made investors chase him. And the only thing that made that possible was being too busy building something people actually wanted.

What do you think of Jesse’s journey?

AI automation experts are selling workflows worth ₹20 lakh for ₹20,000.

The market is hot. Companies are desperate for their service. But most of them are still broke.

And the problem isn't their skill. It's how they price.

Here's the AI Cost Justification Framework that fixes that:

Step 1: Map the process you're automating

Who does it, how long does it take, and how often?
Example: A company has 3 support agents, each spending 6 hours a day on tickets, at ₹400/hour.

Step 2: Calculate what it's costing them annually

6 hours x ₹400 x 3 agents x 300 working days = ₹21.6 lakh/year on one process.

Step 3: Estimate your impact

Your workflow automates 80% of those tickets. That's ₹17.2 lakh saved every year.

Step 4: Price at 20% of annual savings

That's ₹2.8 lakh for one project. The client still gets a 5x return on what they paid you. And you have a number you can defend.

Compare that to the freelancer who thinks:

“I built it in 10 hours, at ₹2000 per hour that’s ₹20,000.”

P.S - If you’re scared of competitors underpricing, find a niche and specialise.

Are you pricing your AI workflows this way, or still charging by the hour?
